metadata.title: Archetype Drift — Three Case Studies with Claude
metadata.purpose: Show students in Nina Harris' Branding and AI course how Claude synthesizes the three canonical archetype-drift cases (Tropicana/Gap/New Coke) into a structured analysis and abstract drift-signature framework.
metadata.audience: Students and practitioners in a university branding course with an AI component
sources: Branding and AI, Chapter 5 — Brand Archetypes (Nina Harris)

beats.narration_text: Aloha — this is Liam, in for Bear. For Nina Harris' course, Branding and AI, here is the question we are running today: Tropicana did not lose its recipe. Gap did not close its stores. New Coke was not bad product. They lost their recognition asset — and that asset is invisible until it is destroyed. We are using Claude to synthesize the three canonical cases and extract the four warning signs that appear before the destruction happens.

props.command: Research the three canonical archetype-drift cases: Tropicana 2009, Gap 2010, New Coke 1985. For each: archetype held, triggering action, customer response timeline, equity impact, recovery move. Then abstract a four-item drift-signature framework.
output: Tropicana: Innocent → packaging stripped recognition asset → $33M sales drop
Gap: Everyman → logo modernized → reverted in 6 days
New Coke: Everyman → formula changed → 79 days, 400K complaint calls
Drift signature: four warning signs abstracted

beats.narration_text: Here is what breaks without a drift-signature framework. Brand changes are made by executives who know what they are doing, with real budgets, real consultancies, and genuine conviction that improvement is happening. The recognition asset is the thing they did not measure because it was invisible to their dashboard. Performance dashboards move slowly relative to brand tracking studies. By the time the damage appears in performance data, the recognition asset is already gone.

props.sparkLine: By the time the dashboard shows it — the asset is gone.

beats.narration_text: The theory from Chapter Five. The recognition asset is the thing a brand's audience cannot articulate but immediately feels when it is removed. It is the orange-and-straw on a Tropicana carton. It is the Helvetica weight on a Gap bag. It is the specific sweetness profile of a Coke formula. Each asset is invisible under normal conditions and only becomes legible under archetype drift — when the brand changes the ONE thing that anchors the recognition pattern. The drift mechanism: internal confidence in improvement is systematically miscalibrated to the recognition asset's fragility.

The centerpiece: three-case comparison table. Tropicana 2009: Innocent archetype, triggered by stripping the orange-and-straw recognition cue from the packaging, customer backlash within two months, thirty-three million dollars in sales drop, recovered by returning to the original packaging. Gap 2010: Everyman archetype, triggered by logo modernization, crowdsourced public backlash peaked in six days, association strength collapsed, reverted in one week. New Coke 1985: Everyman archetype, triggered by a formula change justified by taste tests, four hundred thousand complaint calls over seventy-nine days, identity severed from the audience, recovered by relaunching Coca-Cola Classic. Below the table: the four-item drift signature. Source: Branding and AI, Nina Harris, Chapter Five.

Honesty about what the framework does not do. Returning to the original archetype is not always the right recovery move. The recovery analysis must ask whether the original archetype is still relevant to the current audience. New Coke returned to Classic successfully because the Everyman archetype was still live. A brand whose original archetype has genuinely drifted out of cultural relevance faces a different problem — repositioning, not reversal.

artifactLines: Returning to the original archetype is not always the correct recovery move.
The original archetype must still be relevant to the current audience.
Repositioning is different from reversal — the framework names both but doesn't choose.
The drift-signature warns before the event; it doesn't design the recovery.

beats.narration_text: Let us recap with Claude. Archetype Drift Case Studies. Four lines.

artifactLines: The recognition asset is invisible until it is destroyed — then it is obvious.
Three canonical cases: Tropicana, Gap, New Coke — each a different archetype, same drift mechanism.
The drift-signature framework abstracts four warning signs applicable to any brand.
Performance dashboards move slowly; the brand tracking metric that catches drift is association strength.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Take this prompt into your Claude session: I am analyzing a brand I work with or have studied. Describe the brand to Claude: its archetype, its recognition assets — the specific visual, verbal, or sensory cues that make it immediately recognizable — and any recent changes the brand has made to its identity, visuals, or product. Then ask Claude to run the four-item drift-signature framework against that brand: which of the four warning signs are already present? Which recognition assets are at risk if the current direction continues? Name the asset most at risk. This is the valuable question — not whether your brand will change, but what it will break if it does.

props.command: Run the drift-signature framework on this brand: [BRAND + ARCHETYPE + RECOGNITION ASSETS + RECENT CHANGES]. Which of the four warning signs are already present? Which recognition assets are most at risk if the current direction continues?

beats.narration_text: Archetype Drift — Three Case Studies with Claude. Liam, in for Bear.
